article_title: Transnational governance and the re-centered state: Sustainability or legality?

It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Tim Bartley Department of Sociology, Ohio State University, Columbus, OH, USA Abstract After two decades in which transnational governance of production processes has typically meant volun- tary subscription to privately developed standards, some transnational rulemaking projects are promoting mandatory compliance with law. The emerging timber legality regime is one example of this, and scholars’ efforts to theorize this regime have produced provocative new analyses of interactions between public and private standards. Recent analyses, including those in this issue, predict that the new legality regime will bolster voluntary initiatives that certify sustainable forests. Based on research in Indonesia and China, I argue that this prediction is questionable and that the rise of the timber legality regime could constrict, rather than expand the space for global private authority. Further, I argue that it would not be entirely a bad thing if the legality regime overtook sustainability certiﬁcation. Behind these speciﬁc arguments are general perspectives on how domestic circumstances shape transnational business governance and on the role of states in pluralistic ﬁelds of governance – both issues that are obscured by more architectural approaches. Keywords: corporate social responsibility, forest certiﬁcation, sustainability, timber legality, transnational governance. 1. Introduction The rise of a transnational timber legality regime is a remarkable, path-breaking event in the governance of forests.As activists and scholars have pointed out, a great deal of forest destruction stems from illegal logging, carried out by both small-scale loggers and large timber operators (Global Witness 2007; Tacconi 2007). In the late 1990s, activism and research began drawing international attention to illegal logging. By 2010, activist campaigns, market pressures, and inter-governmental negotiations had congealed into something resembling a transnational timber legality regime, rooted in bilateral agreements, the leveraging of trade to promote forest law enforcement, and, ultimately, in legislative interventions, such as the United States (US) Lacey Act and the European Union (EU) Timber Regulation, both of which penalize the sale of illegally harvested forest products (United States Congress 2008; European Parliament 2010). This nascent legality regime requires importers in the US and EU to exercise due diligence (or due care) of their timber supplies and subjects them to hard penalties (conﬁscation, ﬁnes, or even imprisonment in exceptional circumstances) for illegal timber in their supply chains. In several timber exporting countries, multi-stakeholder dialogues have operationally deﬁned Correspondence: Tim Bartley, Department of Sociology, Ohio State University, Townshend Hall 238, Columbus, OH 43210, USA. Together, these arrangements accomplish things – like transnational “hard” law, extended liability (beyond the boundaries of the ﬁrm), and extraterritorial regulation – that have so often been dismissed as practically or politically impossible. Binding international regulations for forestry appeared to many to have been foreclosed in the 1990s, when timber import bans were treated as incom- patible with the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT)/World Trade Organization (WTO), and the United Nations failed to approve an international forest convention, leading environmentalists and foresters to build private-sector, market-based solutions like the Forest Stewardship Council (FSC) (Bartley 2007). The recent rise of binding timber legality rules, occurring in the midst of private efforts to certify sustainability, leaves us with an important set of puzzles: How did regulatory strategies that had apparently been foreclosed in the 1990s become realities in the 2000s? How should we understand the co-evolution of legality veriﬁca- tion and sustainability certiﬁcation? Contributions by Overdevest and Zeitlin (2014) and Cashore and Stone (2012, 2014) go a long way toward solving these puzzles, and work by Gulbrandsen (2014) provides additional guidance for theorizing private standards and public authority. Overdevest and Zeitlin (hereaf- ter, O&Z) theorize forestry standards through the lens of experimentalist governance. In experi- mentalist governance architectures, local actors (ﬁrms, governments, or others) can respond to policy goals in varied ways, but must report on their activities such that information can be pooled, peer reviewed, and used to revise expectations. The system is “experimentalist” in that it fosters discovery of preferences and possibilities, rather than imposing pre-ordained behavioral rules. O&Z argue that the EU-led system of Forest Law Enforcement, Governance, and Trade (FLEGT) amounts to an experimentalist governance regime, and that, in combination with the US Lacey Act and an upwardly evolving ﬁeld of sustainability certiﬁcation, we are witnessing the construction of a promising “regime complex” in the forestry sector, undergirded by an experi- mentalist architecture. Cashore and Stone (hereafter, C&S) shed light on the sources of this nascent regime with an account of coalitions that came to support it. In the US, a “Baptist-bootlegger” coalition of environmentalists and domestic lumber manufacturers supported the expansion of the Lacey Act. In Indonesia, reformers within government, indigenous rights advocates, and some timber exporting companies all found enough to like about a push for timber legality to eventually support it. But legality standards also pose a conundrum for C&S, because they are weaker than the leading private sustainability standards (especially the FSC’s, but also those of its competi- tor, the Programme for the Endorsement of Forest Certiﬁcation [PEFC]). In a spirit not too distant from O&Z, C&S argue that standard-setting projects can reinforce one another and evolve over time, such that an apparent weakening of standards can lead to stronger standards in the future. Gulbrandsen’s focus is not on legality, but on the relationship between states and certiﬁca- tion initiatives. Like other work on this topic, he shows that states are intimately involved in nominally private orders, but he also shows that governments took different approaches in different arenas (forestry and ﬁshery standards) as they championed standards that were per- ceived as beneﬁcial to favored national industries. These papers sit on the cutting edge of research on transnational business governance interactions, both theoretically and substantively. They provide signiﬁcant answers to Eberlein et al. ’s (2014) questions about mechanisms and outcomes of interactions. The papers on legality veriﬁcation are especially valuable for their fresh observations and sophisticated theoretical frames. Re-centered state T. Bartley © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 94 Page 3: It is in this spirit of appreciation that I cast doubt upon some aspects of these authors’ arguments. I focus on the question of how the rise of the timber legality regime will shape transnational business governance interactions in the forestry sector, taking some guidance from Gulbrandsen’s approach. In particular, I question the argument that the legality regime is likely to support the expansion of private forest certiﬁcation as we know it. This prediction is made, albeit in slightly different ways, by both O&Z and C&S, and both sets of authors are hopeful that legality veriﬁcation will not marginalize higher-bar, potentially experimentalist efforts to certify sustainable forestry. This prediction is questionable, and the hope of saving a place for certiﬁ- cation may be both overly optimistic and misplaced. This critique is rooted in my own research on how global forestry standards are put into practice, especially in two different national settings – Indonesia and China. Between 2008 and 2010, I conducted 35 interviews (plus other informal discussions) in Indonesia, with auditors, non-governmental organizations (NGOs), managers of international and domestic companies, foresters, and consultants. These were mainly conducted in the greater Jakarta area, with several in East Kalimantan and Yogyakarta. Between 2010 and 2011, I conducted 17 interviews (and a number of informal discussions) with actors in the forestry/timber sector in China, again including auditors, NGOs, managers, and others, mainly centered in Beijing. This ﬁeld research is supplemented with an examination of the public summaries of audit reports for all forest management units assessed for FSC certiﬁcation in these two countries from 1999 to 2010, as well as secondary sources. As C&S discuss, Indonesia and China are two crucial national cases that occupy somewhat different positions in the emerging legality regime. Indonesia is a leading producer and exporter of tropical forest products, including pulp and paper and sawnwood/plywood (International Tropical Timber Organization 2008). Indonesian forests have also been at the center of contro- versy and activism over illegal logging, which has occurred through small-scale timber extrac- tion, large-scale disregard of concession boundaries, and illegal clearing of forests for agriculture. Like several other countries, Indonesia has signed a Voluntary Partnership Agreement (VPA) with the EU to crack down on illegal logging and create a new domestic system for verifying legality. China’s position in the global forest products industry is largely as a processor – manufacturing a large amount of furniture, ﬂooring, and paper for export. Exports of furniture from China to the US nearly quadrupled in volume from 1997 to 2007 (Gregg & Porges 2008), and the Chinese furniture industry has often been charged with driving demand for illegal timber from Indonesia, Russia, Burma, and Malaysia (Dauvergne & Lister 2011). China is in a pivotal position in the emerging legality regime, and, as O&Z mention, much depends on the Chinese government’s evolving plans for tracking the legality of timber imports.1 Beyond the forestry case, this article carries two implications for theorizing transnational governance. First, it underscores the importance of a close, substantive engagement with the domestic political and economic contexts in which transnational business governance is imple- mented. It is tempting to treat developing countries as “areas of limited statehood” in which private governance essentially ﬁlls a void (Börzel & Risse 2010),but a closer look reveals numerous domestic structures that frustrate, amplify, or reconﬁgure transnational business governance initiatives. Second, this article sharpens the debate about what is and is not unique about states in pluralist ﬁelds of governance. In many cases, transnational business governance initiatives seek to “navigate around” or alter national legal regimes (Eberlein et al. 2014). The rise of transnational governance does not mean the decline of state regulation, but rather, that “state regulators are increasingly embedded in and interplay with many other regulatory actors” (Djelic & Sahlin-Andersson 2006, p. 377). It is tempting, then, to treat the state as just one of many relevant Re-centered state T. Bartley © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 95 Page 4: actors and institutional structures in ﬁelds of transnational governance. This would be a mistake, as states are unique in their capacity to shape market access on a large scale and institutionalize the rights of citizens and ﬁrms within their borders. (For the latter reason, states are also key sites of political struggle, unlikely to be surpassed in this regard by transnational arenas.) What is promising about the timber legality regime, I argue, is that it asks states to play these roles, while private certiﬁcation systems pretend that non-state actors can serve as effective substitutes. Rather than assessing a broader regime complex, I focus on the speciﬁc issue of the timber legality regime and its intersection with certiﬁcation. To get a complete picture of the regime complex for forests, one would also need to examine programs focused on global warming, including carbon markets and the United Nations’ program on Reducing Emissions from Deforestation and Forest Degradation (REDD). These too are reshaping the agenda, interacting with transnational business governance initiatives, and opening up new projects and debates within particular countries. But by honing in on the interaction between legal and sustainable forestry initiatives, much can be learned. 2. Will legality veriﬁcation bolster or undermine sustainability certiﬁcation? As aptly described by C&S and O&Z, the timber legality regime is an outcome of interaction, competition, and learning in the world of forestry standards, and it is potentially transformative of future forms of transnational governance. C&S recognize that the effects could be modest or even perverse – as countries could weaken their laws, or because “focusing on legal compliance might take pressure off of high standard global certiﬁcation programs or broader sustainable forest management and ecosystem protection policies” (2014, p. 66). O&Z similarly recognize the danger that “both customers and suppliers [will] shift their energies towards meeting less demanding legal requirements” (2014, p. 39). Yet both sets of authors set aside these possibilities in order to develop the opposite predic- tion – that the growth of legality standards can foster the growth and effectiveness of private certiﬁcation. C&S, especially in their initial work on this topic, suggest, “legality veriﬁcation provides a prerequisite for global forest certiﬁcation” (2012, p. 20) and has “signiﬁcant potential for reinforcing . . . global private certiﬁcation” (2012, p. 21). In recent work, they suggest that the legality regime increases support for transnational business governance authority writ large and should gradually allow for the kind of substantive improvements that private certiﬁcation requires (2014, pp. 64–66). O&Z similarly predict a positive and generative interaction between legality veriﬁcation and sustainability certiﬁcation, potentially meaning that “a more coherent transnational governance regime can be expected to emerge, cutting across conventional dis- tinctions between public and private authority” (2014, p. 32). In both cases, the authors argue that this bolstering of forest certiﬁcation is not automatic, but instead rests upon continued scrutiny and demands for accountability. But both sets of authors see these trajectories as consistent with past experience in this ﬁeld. Such trajectories are also consistent with these scholars’ larger theoretical perspectives, which emphasize capacities for collective monitoring and deliberation (Sabel & Zeitlin 2008) and the ability of reformers to convert opposition into broad-scale support (Cashore et al. 2004). This shared prediction rests on three accounts of how the legality regime will support the growth and effectiveness of forest certiﬁcation. One is focused on risk management, another on the infrastructure for certiﬁcation, and a third on market segmentation and coalition-formation. Below, I review and contextualize these accounts, pointing out several problematic aspects as revealed by a closer look at domestic circumstances. Re-centered state T. Certiﬁcation as risk management The legality regime could support sustainability certiﬁcation if the latter is used as a symbol of the “due diligence” or “due care” that timber importers are expected to exercise under the EU Timber Regulation and the Lacey Act. O&Z suggest that, for some ﬁrms, certiﬁcation can be “a cost-effective alternative to creating and administering their own free-standing risk manage- ment systems” (2014, p. 38). This would be most important for ﬁrms importing from countries (e.g. Russia, Brazil, China, India) outside the “green lane” provided by a FLEGT-recognized timber licensing system. The EU Timber Regulation does state, “certiﬁcation or other third party veriﬁed schemes that include veriﬁcation of compliance with applicable legislation may be used in the risk assessment procedure” (European Parliament 2010, p. 25) (an example of what Eberlein et al. [2014] call “conditional rule referencing”). In the US, certiﬁcation could expand and become institutionalized if ﬁrms use it to manage uncertainty (Cashore & Stone 2012), and especially if the Lacey Act’s implementing guidelines move to “recognize private forest certiﬁ- cation schemes as evidence of legality”, as O&Z put it (2014, p. 32). Yet it is doubtful that FSC or other high-bar certiﬁcation of forest management will become a widespread sign of due diligence/due care. For forest management companies, certiﬁcation is arduous, costly, and rarely undertaken unless a valued buyer expresses a speciﬁc demand for certiﬁed wood. This is clear when looking at attempts to increase the rate of FSC certiﬁcation, especially in developing countries. Because the FSC’s standards are demanding and the cost of achieving and maintaining certiﬁcation can be high, many of the ﬁrms that have participated in projects to expand certiﬁcation (led by the World Wildlife Fund’s [WWFs] Global Forest and Trade Network, The Tropical Forest Foundation, and others) have given up, failed pre- assessments, or decided it was ultimately not worth the cost.2 Those who succeed usually face new challenges and costs over time, as they receive new “corrective action requests” in follow-up audits. Subsidizing the cost of certiﬁcation clearly increases adoption, but this requires retailers, NGOs, or governments to contribute large amounts of money. The Borneo Initiative, a project funded by Dutch companies and government, contributes two dollars per hectare to certify forests in Indonesia, amounting to a “partial” subsidy of $6.7 million for 31 participating ﬁrms.3 If managing risk under the legality regime is the primary goal of retailers and manufacturers, it is unlikely that they will make the substantial commitments required for certiﬁcation, especially as there are other tools they can use that are less costly and less open to public scrutiny than FSC (or even PEFC) certiﬁcation. First, internal monitoring and tracing programs allow ﬁrms to keep these activities under their control and are feasible for large ﬁrms that already have sourcing ofﬁces in key regions of the world. Research on other standards has shown that large retailers and brands have often spurned external certiﬁcation in favor of in-house monitoring systems (Raynolds 2009; Bartley 2010; Fransen 2011); ﬁrms subject to domestic labor market regulation have built internal structures to manage risk (Edelman 1992). Second, retailers and manufacturers that do not develop their own tracing programs can draw on a growing set of services that are offered by third parties, but are not equivalent to forest certiﬁcation. Most prominent are legality audits offered by the Rainforest Alliance, Société Générale de Surveillance (SGS), and Bureau Veritas (all of which also offer FSC certiﬁcation) and supply chain tracing programs offered by The Forest Trust (TFT, previously called Tropical Forest Trust). With the exception of the Rainforest Alliance, private legality veriﬁcation pro- grams do not publicly release summaries of audit reports (Greenpeace 2008), marking a sig- niﬁcant difference from forest certiﬁcation. TFT, developed to serve European ﬁrms facing scrutiny over illegal and unsustainable timber, provides supply chain tracing support for Re-centered state T. Bartley © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 97 Page 6: importers and helps forest management companies prepare for FSC certiﬁcation. It does so on a conﬁdential, contractual basis and allows participating ﬁrms to use the TFT logo in their marketing. Though potentially a step toward FSC certiﬁcation, there is a risk of it serving as a less transparent substitute. In Indonesia, most of the natural forest concessionaires that have worked with TFT have not achieved FSC certiﬁcation.4 TFT’s attempts to gradually improve forest management certainly have virtues in opaque, conﬂictual, and ﬁnancially unstable set- tings that are infertile ground for high-bar certiﬁcation.5 But TFT lacks the transparency and multi-stakeholder participation that make forest certiﬁcation – and especially the FSC – attrac- tive to scholars of experimentalist governance and non-state authority. For ﬁrms looking to demonstrate due diligence/care without publicly exposing themselves, the conﬁdential nature of most veriﬁcation and tracing initiatives should be attractive. Risk management in the timber legality regime could fuel one particular form of certiﬁca- tion: To send positive signals to their customers in the US and Europe, manufacturing ﬁrms may seek FSC “chain of custody” certiﬁcation, which is less costly and difﬁcult. This may seem like an expansion of forest certiﬁcation, but it has no necessary link to greater amounts of forest area being certiﬁed. Chain of custody certiﬁcation means that a factory tracks certiﬁed and uncertiﬁed inputs, but a factory can be certiﬁed even if it is not currently using any certiﬁed wood. Chain of custody certiﬁcation has greatly outpaced growth in the area of certiﬁed forests. From 2007 to 2012, the number of FSC chain of custody certiﬁcates worldwide increased by roughly 280%, while the total amount of FSC certiﬁed forest area increased by only around 65% (Forest Stewardship Council 2010; Forest Stewardship Council 2012). In China, FSC chain of custody certiﬁcates increased from less than 1000 in early 2010 to over 2800 by mid-2013. Observers there note that factories commonly get chain of custody certiﬁcation without actually using certiﬁed wood.6 As explained by one participant,“a retailer might just ask a supplier to get certiﬁed but not demand the [certiﬁed] products themselves.”6 Linking this to the legality regime, another observer suggested, “Customers asked for chain of custody and stopped there. They may call that due diligence, but this is not really due diligence.”7 To be sure, chain of custody certiﬁcation does not fully meet the EU Timber Regulation’s requirements for a due diligence system, which should provide information on the species and legality of all timber being used. But it may be used as a low cost form of “symbolic compliance”(Edelman 1992) in the hope that it is perceived as mitigating risk or increasing traceability. Overall, the timber legality regime is likely to increase demand for assurances of various sorts, but these are unlikely to look like forest management certiﬁcation as we currently know it. 2.2. Improving the infrastructure Legality veriﬁcation could raise the baseline level of forestry practices and provide a ﬁrmer infrastructure for forest governance, thus increasing the chances for interested producers to meet the higher bar of sustainability certiﬁcation. At a general level, this is a reasonable assumption that responds to research on barriers to certiﬁcation. In most circumstances, getting certiﬁed requires companies to make a variety of changes, “sometimes minor, but sometimes involving radical departures from the previous management style in a region” (Nussbaum & Simula 2004, p. 19). This includes implementing reduced impact logging, protecting high conservation value forest areas, addressing community conﬂicts, and formalizing numerous policies and manage- ment plans. As one consultant in Indonesia put it, “if you suddenly put in front of a company here the entire list of things they need to do [for certiﬁcation], it’s daunting. . .. But as they begin to comply with legality, they’re beginning to do other things.”8 Raising the ﬂoor should make it easier for those ﬁrms that wish to climb to a higher level to do so. Re-centered state T. Bartley © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 98 Page 7: Delving further into how this infrastructure will be improved, C&S emphasize the traceabil- ity of timber supplies. Even ﬁrms that have sought to promote certiﬁcation in their supply chains have sometimes found it difﬁcult to trace their full supply chain. Ikea promotes FSC, but nevertheless struggles to identify the ultimate source of wood that goes through a series of timber traders on the Russian-Chinese borders to its suppliers in China.9 Tracking the chain of custody of certiﬁed timber is highly imperfect as well. Auditors in Indonesia and China have admitted that this system is open to abuse, especially because of the non-transparency of these audits, their inconsistency across different parts of the supply chain, and the lack of centralized data pooling – with one auditor calling the chain of custody system “a house of cards.”10 Technological improvements could certainly help (Auld et al. 2010), but there is a long way to go before one can speak of reliable, large-scale, transparent tracking of forest products, even those that are certiﬁed. C&S believe that legality veriﬁcation will, in effect, subsidize the costs of supply chain tracking and, thus, support beyond-compliance activities (2014, pp. 64–66). O&Z’s account focuses not only on traceability, but also on law: “By harmonizing inconsis- tencies, ﬁlling gaps, and resolving conﬂicts in domestic law, including those concerning custom- ary rights of indigenous communities, the revised legal standards produced through the VPA process will greatly facilitate auditing of compliance by individual FMUs with national legal requirements, which is a core element of all private certiﬁcation schemes” (2014, p. 39). This issue is especially important in countries with conﬂicts over land rights, ambiguous boundaries of forest concessions, and lawless cultures of production. Indonesia is a prime example of how an ambiguous, inconsistent, and contested set of land use rights can inhibit the growth of certiﬁcation (Bartley 2010), but conﬂicts over community/customary land rights are also common in Brazil, Canada, Cambodia, and elsewhere; and legal frameworks in many tropical forest countries are “antiquated, inadequate, underfunded, and poorly enforced” (Nasi & Frost 2009, p. 3). To the extent that the timber legality regime could help to make land use and forest governance clearer and fairer, it could create a socio-legal infrastructure that would be far more conducive to sustainable forestry and the certiﬁcation thereof. These accounts of the timber legality regime’s possible effects on the information and legal infrastructure for certiﬁcation are useful, however, especially on the issue of legal reform, they are more optimistic than the experience to date warrants. There is little doubt that the legality regime will add some transparency and traceability to timber production. In Indonesia, the Timber Legality Assurance System (or SVLK, Sistem Veriﬁkasi Legalitas Kayu) means that private auditors, approved by the national accreditation agency, will scrutinize all 300-plus forest concessionaires and thousands of timber processing companies, not simply those that choose to get certiﬁed. Auditors should check licenses and harvesting plans in concessions and assess whether processors can trace timber back to legal forestry operations. Summaries of the audits are posted on the Ministry of Forestry’s website. Even if auditing and reporting are imperfect, this system will signiﬁcantly increase the amount of information available. The FLEGT-required multi-stakeholder process for “deﬁning legality” was also promising in Indonesia. The group included representatives from the Ministry of Forestry, forest management companies, auditors, international NGOs (The Nature Conservancy and Greenpeace), and domestic NGOs focused on environmental issues (Telapak, Forest Watch, WALHI) and indig- enous communities (AMAN). The standards that this group produced are an important achieve- ment, but they do not immediately address the problem of inconsistent laws or deal directly with customary rights of communities. Though these issues were pressed by domestic NGOs in the negotiations, they were addressed only in minimal ways in the ﬁnal standard, which puts all of Re-centered state T. This does not negate the future possibility for the kind of broad scale evaluation of forest tenure and rights that activists and scholars have called for (Colchester et al. 2003), but it does mean that legality veriﬁcation mainly takes the existing socio-legal environ- ment as a given. The Indonesian experience shows that even when multi-stakeholder dialogue on deﬁning legality is productive, the problem of contradictory and ambiguous laws can persist. My point is not to minimize the importance of this multi-stakeholder process or the resulting standard. Indeed, I will argue that this type of domestic standard-setting arena is, in some ways, more conducive to the kind of long-term contentious politics that can lead to signiﬁcant legal reform. At its best, the international timber legality regime might support this long process, but its short-term effect on legal conﬁgurations – and, thus, on forest certiﬁcation – is doubtful. 2.3. Segregating markets A third account focuses on how restricting the market for illegal timber will affect “legitimate” and “illegitimate” producers and build coalitions of reformers. Illegal logging and unsustainable logging have persisted because they can be highly proﬁtable (Putz & Nasi 2009). Both C&S and O&Z express hope that the new legality regime will cause the market for illegal timber to collapse. Stronger controls on access to the US and EU markets should help to weed out illegitimate players, thus, “reducing a major source of cost pressure on legitimate timber opera- tions” (Overdevest & Zeitlin 2014, p. 39), who are then expected to engage in willful, good faith reporting, review, and benchmarking. C&S argue that legality veriﬁcation can help to build a global coalition of ﬁrms, governments, and NGOs that can beneﬁt from higher prices and stronger standards, while excluding “those undertaking illegal logging practices,” as well as corrupt ofﬁcials, low-end manufacturers, and highly price-sensitive consumers (2012, p. 19). Within timber exporting countries, incentives for veriﬁed legal sources are argued to contribute to “good forest governance” coalitions (2012, 2014). If these coalitions take an expansive view that includes “good forest management practices,” not merely “good private property rights,” then this could indeed provide the kind of organized domestic support for forest certiﬁcation that scholars have found to be crucial elsewhere (Cashore et al. 2004; Espach 2009). Although the timber legality regime is likely to reshape markets, the idea that it will weed out the bad actors and reward the good ones rests on a distinction between legitimate and illegiti- mate ﬁrms that becomes hard to sustain as one looks more closely at timber operations. Both “sustainable” and “illegal” timber can easily coexist in the same company, supply chain, or product. Many of the leading “good” actors have also been implicated in activities that are, at best, legally questionable, and at worst, illegal. Several examples from Indonesia illustrate how fuzzy the line between legitimate and ille- gitimate timber operators can be. Among the select group of ﬁrms in Indonesia that have had forest management units certiﬁed by the FSC, many have also struggled to meet basic criteria for legality. Audit reports reveal that in the process of getting FSC certiﬁed, four ﬁrms were cited for major nonconformities with FSC criteria for respecting national law or preventing illegal logging.12 PT Intracawood was initially cited for failing to control illegal logging in its concession, for purchasing logs that were most likely illegal, and for not respecting customary land use rights of communities. Another company, Sumalindo Lestari Jaya, was once held up as a forbearer of sustainable, traceable, and legal Indonesian timber (Clifford 2003) and called “ahead of the curve” in achieving FSC certiﬁcation for one of its concessions.13 But in 2010, two executives were arrested when the company was accused of “receiving 3,000 pieces of illegally logged timber” (Tempo 2010). A court later found the executives not guilty, but the case remains a cautionary tale of how intertwined the worlds of “sustainable” and “illegal” timber may be. Perum Perhutani, the state owned ﬁrm that controls Javanese forests, was the ﬁrst forest to be FSC certiﬁed in Indonesia. But in 2001, the certiﬁcate was suspended in the midst of a rising tide of illegal logging and violent conﬂict between the company’s armed forest guards and local people (Donovan 2001) – part of a long and violent history of conﬂict with forest dwelling communities (Peluso 1992). Through efforts to disarm its forest guards and improve community relations, the company succeeded in getting several teak forests certiﬁed again in 2011, but it retains the legacy of “a company that’s killed a lot of illegal loggers.”14 The twisted historical links between legality, illegality, and sustainability are dra- matic in this case.15 To be sure, there are other ﬁrms that are more deeply intertwined with illegal logging that those described above, but except at the extremes, it may be difﬁcult to identify them. Tackling the problem of illegal logging does not mean driving one set of producers out of the market and rewarding another, it requires a weeding out of illegal timber from the supply chains of main- stream ﬁrms, and even some that have been recognized as “green.” It is possible that reducing illegal activities could, over time, make it easier for some ﬁrms to keep themselves “clean” and participate whole-heartedly in good governance coalitions. Indeed, there may be opportunities for coalitions to arise without assuming a simple segregation of “good” and “bad” actors. But a deeper analysis of the market and political conditions for this is necessary. With a decline in the extraordinarily high rates of proﬁt once available in logging in Southeast Asia, we might expect an exit of investors who saw timber (legal or illegal) as a quick proﬁt opportunity, rather than a long-term investment. An analysis of investment opportunities ought to be coupled with an account that clariﬁes the historical and political context of domes- tic “good forest governance” coalitions. Gellert’s (2010) analysis shows that good forest gover- nance coalitions in Indonesia are neither new nor mainly domestic. The World Bank and the International Monetary Fund (IMF) have, for several decades, pushed a “good governance” agenda in Indonesian forests, rooted initially in a neoliberal attempt at “the insulation of markets from collective social demands” (Gellert 2010, p. 542), but this has repeatedly faltered against the weight of the timber industry and its “industrialist growth” model. Over time, good governance advocates have progressively, but selectively, incorporated social justice ideas from what Gellert calls the “NGO bottom-up social reform model” (p. 545). An important question is whether the legality regime could help forge a more robust coalition between good gover- nance and social justice reformers in Indonesia. Put differently, it is important to ask about emergent coalitions of property rights and human rights advocates, not just Baptists (environ- mentalists) and bootleggers (ﬁrms). Addressing this question requires an analysis that is more attentive to domestic political circumstances than to global private authority. C&S have done a great service by bringing domestic good governance coalitions into the analysis of the legality regime, but it will take more than simple economic projections of price changes (2012) or invocations of “historical institutionalist processes” (2014) to unpack the shape and signiﬁcance of domestic reform movements.
