Making sense of the state How might the timber legality regime shake up the relationship between states and transnational business governance? Existing theories focus on how states and inter-governmental organizations shape transnational governance arenas by supporting, endorsing, or orchestrating nominally private orders (see Abbott & Snidal 2009). Gulbrandsen provides an astute addition, showing how the actions of governmental and inter-governmental organizations facilitated the growth of forest and ﬁsheries certiﬁcation in different ways. His conclusion that the rise of private certiﬁcation is “not indicative of less government involvement but rather of public-private governance interactions” (p. 89) is undeniable, and his analysis points to important differences between governments that are, for a particular product, mainly importers, exporters, or both. The timber legality regime presents a different conﬁguration of public and private orders: states in major import markets directly create and enforce import restrictions, while also incen- tivizing or endorsing private systems of veriﬁcation and risk management. Inter-governmental agreements (like VPAs) oblige states in exporting countries to develop new legality licensing procedures, typically involving veriﬁcation by state-accredited private auditing organizations. In a sense, the regime means that importing states are using public and private means to enforce the laws of exporting states. While it is not a purely state-to-state regime, the timber legality regime dramatically re-centers the state. To theorize the consequences of this re-centering, one can both borrow from and extend Gulbrandsen’s account. Gulbrandsen shows that Nordic governments, with a major stake in the global seafood industry, challenged the legitimacy of private rulemaking by the Marine Stew- ardship Council. The timber legality regime could spur an even stronger reaction, giving export- ing country governments a strong incentive to inﬂuence international expectations and to control practices and standards within their borders. With favored national industries poten- tially losing access to large markets, export-promoting governments can no longer afford to leave the practice of regulation up to private actors. As I discuss further, this could, in some circum- stances, constrict the space for “global private authority” rather than expand it, as C&S expect. While Gulbrandsen limits his account of the state to its role in shaping standards, a fuller understanding of the timber legality regime is possible if one considers the state’s unique capacities to control market access on a large scale and to institutionalize the rights of citizens. While ﬁrms can shape market access, it would take a high degree of cooperation among com- petitors to do so systematically. State policies minimize these collective action problems. Although states are constrained in important ways by WTO rules, these constraints are not completely prohibitive, as O&Z point out. In addition – and important for assessing the legality regime within timber exporting countries – states are essential to the granting and enforcement of rights to citizens. Obviously there is great variation in the extent to which this occurs, but no private actor – corporations, NGOs, or certiﬁcation initiatives – has the same sort of potential. With these points of orientation, I proceed by ﬁrst looking at how the re-centering of the state could constrict, rather than expand the space for private authority, with particular attention to the Chinese case. Then, in the conclusion, I extend the discussion of the state’s unique capacities to consider whether a greater focus on timber legality might be a good thing, even if it weakened private sustainability certiﬁcation. 3.1. States and the space for global private authority In China, the rise of the legality regime coincided with intensiﬁed state controls over FSC certiﬁcation. There are signs that the Chinese government will shift from its initial recalcitrance (Lawson & MacFaul 2010) to develop a legality veriﬁcation system (see Overdevest & Zeitlin Re-centered state T. Bartley © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 102 Page 11: 2014, Cashore & Stone 2014), but it has also shifted from cautiously supporting FSC certiﬁcation to directly challenging it, by promoting its own domestically-driven certiﬁcation scheme and reducing the space for FSC certiﬁcation. While the causal link between the legality regime and this orientation to sustainability certiﬁcation is not ironclad, it is unlikely that it is a pure coincidence. One Chinese NGO representative noted, in diagnosing the shift to a combative posture, that “the Chinese government came to understand that certiﬁcation tools are very important in trade negotiations and forest-related dialogues with other countries”.16 The Chinese State Forestry Administration (SFA) supported the initiation of FSC certiﬁca- tion of forest management in China in 2000–2001, but eventually, actors within the SFA also led the development of a domestic alternative – China Forest Certiﬁcation Council (CFCC) – with auditing to be done by a Chinese certiﬁcation body, Zhonglin Tianhe. It is not unusual for governments to sponsor forest certiﬁcation programs, but in this case, government agencies have also tightened requirements for FSC-accredited certiﬁers and supportive NGOs. The Certiﬁca- tion and Accreditation Administration of China (CNCA) introduced new rules in 2003 that required certiﬁers working in China to meet certain capital, personnel, and location require- ments, and to have the scope of their activity approved (State Council of the People’s Republic of China 2013). But the CNCA lacked clear procedures for approving forestry as part of a certiﬁer’s scope of activity, making this a de facto restriction.17 FSC-International and CNCA developed an agreement in 2007 that let foreign certiﬁers resume work by cooperating with local certiﬁcation bodies. But in 2010, CNCA announced that it would strengthen supervision of FSC certiﬁers, in essence tightening enforcement of the 2003 regulation and nullifying the 2007 agreement. This put FSC certiﬁcation in China in a precarious and uncertain state. The Rain- forest Alliance, GFA Certiﬁcation, Scientiﬁc Certiﬁcation Systems, and the Soil Association ceased doing new forest management audits and shifted existing work to the large certiﬁers (SGS and Bureau Veritas) that met the administrative requirements. But even these had not been approved by CNCA to include forestry within their scope of activities. Only Zhonglin Tianhe – the auditor for the domestic forest certiﬁcation system – managed to get that approval.18 This fed into a discourse that the FSC is “illegal” in China – both because of the ambiguous status of its certiﬁcation bodies and because the FSC itself was not yet registered as an organization in China.19 The space for NGOs to support the FSC has also become more constricted. WWF’s Chinese ofﬁce has helped to move companies toward FSC certiﬁcation, but its required sponsor, the SFA, at one point asked WWF China to curtail this aspect of its work.20 Beyond pointing out the challenges for transnational governance in the Chinese context, these events suggest that the re-centering of the state entailed in the legality regime could, in at least some settings, reduce, rather than expand the space for existing forms of global private authority. While it is possible that the Chinese case is exceptional, this should be investigated, rather than assumed, perhaps by examining the current politics of certiﬁcation in Russia, countries in the Congo basin, Vietnam, Cambodia, and other settings where enhanced scrutiny of illegal logging could lead states to seek stronger control over the larger corpus of forestry standards. One plausible prediction is that states may seek to control scrutiny of forestry within their borders, but that weaker states will have a less capacity than stronger states to do so, perhaps preserving greater space for private authority there. 4. Conclusion: Why this is not entirely a bad thing As the 1990s are remembered for the failures of inter-governmental efforts and the rise of private certiﬁcation initiatives, the past ten years will be remembered for the rise of a timber legality Re-centered state T. Bartley © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 103 Page 12: regime – and the partial re-centering of the state that this represents. Analyses by O&Z and C&S provide insightful accounts of how this happened. But both sets of authors stretch their analyses when they argue that this legality regime will ultimately reinforce existing sustainable forestry certiﬁcation initiatives.Although any prediction of this sort is highly uncertain, there are reasons to believe that the timber legality regime could lead to a decrease in the prevalence of forest certiﬁcation as we know it and a reconﬁguration of the role of public and private actors in the ﬁeld of forestry standards. There is some evidence that the demands of ﬁrms have already been re-oriented to legality over sustainability. One forestry consultant in Indonesia noted that importers are already more interested in timber origin and legality than in FSC certiﬁcation.21 A trend in that direction could lead to a signiﬁcant decline in the market for certiﬁcation. It is hard to imagine the disappearance of some brands and retailers that want to differentiate themselves through “beyond compliance” green credentials, such as FSC or PEFC certiﬁcation. But it is easy to imagine many other organizations prioritizing traceability, reframing“sustainability” as“legal and responsible sourc- ing,” and accepting third party veriﬁcation of legality as analogous or equivalent to forest certiﬁcation.22 There is also some evidence that a focus on the low bar of legality may lead auditors to informally accept a lower standard of performance for certiﬁcation (Cerutti et al. 2011), thus, undermining standards in the ﬁeld, even if not in the market. Overall, I have pointed to some conditions under which legality veriﬁcation could undermine sustainability certiﬁca- tion, but there is certainly room for more comprehensive analyses of the conditions under which a re-centering of the state enhances or diminishes the prominence and integrity of transnational business governance. From the perspective of improving global forestry practices over time, it might not be an entirely bad thing if the legality regime did undermine forest certiﬁcation as we know it. Although C&S are right that legality standards are weaker than sustainability standards, the legality regime has an important set of virtues that are overlooked in their analysis: it forces a more realistic accounting of the relationship between states, standards, and forest management practices than does forest certiﬁcation. Forest certiﬁcation was founded on the idea of using private actors to do what neither governments nor inter-governmental organizations were willing or able to do. This notion of “bypassing the state” has turned out to be mythical. Domestic political circumstances greatly shape – whether positively or negatively – the rate and meaning of certiﬁcation (Cashore et al. 2004; Nebel et al. 2005; Bartley 2010). While the certi- ﬁcation model did not entirely deny the state a role, it made states background factors, which might mediate the inﬂuence of transnational pressure and supply chain demand. Set aside were the ways in which government policies directly shape forest use, through land tenure or the designation of forest uses, for instance. Furthermore, forest certiﬁcation calls on private actors to solve problems – like land tenure and community rights – that they lack the authority to address, at least in a meaningful and durable fashion. Here, the unique capacity of the state to institu- tionalize rights is important to keep in mind. The timber legality regime explicitly recognizes the role of law and policy in shaping forest management, and it potentially deepens channels for domestic reform, though productive reform is far from guaranteed. As seen in the Indonesian case, operational deﬁnitions of “legal- ity” may tend to sidestep difﬁcult issues of land tenure and community rights. Still, the legality regime may help to create spaces for these negotiations, especially in countries subject to FLEGT and its requirements for multi-stakeholder dialogue. The FSC was supposed to be accompanied by negotiations for country- or region-speciﬁc tailoring of standards, but this has not occurred in many key tropical forest countries (including Indonesia and most of those in Africa).
